title: Bulk report - Week 17
summary:
Capesize
A largely easier end to the week for the big ships with the May Day holiday weekend here and this despite the paper trades trying to stimulate the physical market. There had been hopes of improvement in the Atlantic for front haul at least but most of these cargoes have now been cleared out along with transatlantic cargoes. There remained a few north Atlantic vessels and a few ballasters in the frame.

children.title: Capesize
children.summary: A largely easier end to the week for the big ships with the May Day holiday weekend here and this despite the paper trades trying to stimulate the physical market. There had been hopes of improvement in the Atlantic for front haul at least but most of these cargoes have now been cleared out along with transatlantic cargoes.

children.title: Panamax
children.summary: The north Atlantic market remained under severe pressure, with new business scarce tonnage building a more or less global long weekend approaching. Transatlantic rates for BPI types were significantly under $10,000 daily with many suggesting levels were realistic under $9,000 daily and front haul rates dropping sharply.

children.title: Supramax
children.summary: The Atlantic market softened towards the end of the week tonnage generally plentiful. US Gulf rates were mixed when the week began with a 63,000-dwt 2015-built reportedly fixed at $24,750 daily delivery Cristobal back to Singapore-Japan range and a 58,000-dwt 2007-built done at $20,000 daily from the US Gulf for a similar run.

children.title: Handysize
children.summary: The slide for all routes became evident today with east coast South America taking the biggest hit. Most Atlantic charterers appeared to be happy to wait and roll into next week in the expectation that rates will be considerably lower.
